text: Iran is set to lift its ban on women attending sports events. Deputy Sports Minister Abdolhamid Ahmadi has said that a plan to allow "women and families" to enter sports stadiums will come into effect next year. It remains unclear which games women will be permitted to attend. Following the Islamic Revolution in 1979, Iranian authorities banned women from attending men's sports events, deeming mixed crowds to be un-Islamic. The ban was briefly lifted for volleyball matches under the moderate President Mohammad Khatami, but was reinstated in 2005.

text: The ban on women attending sports events in Iran is set to be lifted for some indoor events, according to a top Iranian sports official. However, it is unclear which specific games women will be able to attend. The ban on women attending men's sports events was imposed after the Islamic Revolution in 1979. The restrictions have faced criticism from international sports officials, including FIFA President Sepp Blatter.

text: Survival stories at sea are not just for Hollywood, they happen in real life too, and they are usually incredible tales of human endurance, ingenuity, resourcefulness, and often, luck. For example, Louis Jordan set off on his 35-foot sailboat from South Carolina in late January, and was found 200 miles off the North Carolina coast by the Houston Express, a German-flagged container ship. He survived by rationing water and using laundry to trap and scoop up fish. Jose Salvador Alvarenga claims he survived a year adrift in the Pacific Ocean by eating raw birds, sea turtles and drinking rainwater, and when it wasn't available, his own urine. Ron Ingraham, a fisherman from Hawaii, survived 12 days at sea after his 25-foot sailboat was hit by a storm, surviving on what he could catch from the sea. Adrian Vasquez, 18, was adrift for 26 days, after the engine of his small boat died. He survived by eating raw fish and drinking rainwater. Hiromitsu Shinkawa survived the 2011 tsunami in Japan by clinging to the roof of his house, which was swept nine miles out to sea. He was eventually spotted waving a self-made red flag.

entities.text: Ron Ingraham, a fisherman from Hawaii

text:
The news article discusses several incredible stories of individuals who were stranded at sea for extended periods of time and managed to survive against all odds. These stories resemble the plot of movies like "All Is Lost" and "Life of Pi," but they are real-life accounts of survival.

One of the stories is about Louis Jordan, who set off on his sailboat in late January from South Carolina. His boat capsized, and he suffered injuries, but he managed to survive by rationing water and catching fish. After being stranded for several weeks, he was rescued by crew members on a German-flagged container ship.

Another story is about Jose Salvador Alvarenga, who disappeared after setting off from Mexico's Pacific coast in late 2012. He survived over a year adrift in the Pacific Ocean, drinking rainwater and eating sea turtles. He eventually arrived at a remote atoll in the Marshall Islands and was rescued.

The article also mentions Ron Ingraham, a fisherman from Hawaii, who was stranded at sea for 12 days after his sailboat was damaged in bad weather. He was eventually found weak and dehydrated but alive.

Additionally, the article discusses Adrian Vasquez, who was stranded at sea for 26 days after his boat's engine died. His two companions died during the ordeal, and he survived by eating raw fish and drinking rainwater. He was rescued by fishermen near the Galapagos Islands.

Lastly, the article mentions Hiromitsu Shinkawa, who survived the devastating tsunami that struck Japan in 2011. He was swept away by the tsunami but managed to hold onto the roof of a floating house. He was eventually rescued by a Japanese Maritime Self-Defense Force destroyer.

These stories highlight the incredible resilience and resourcefulness of individuals who find themselves in life-threatening situations at sea. Despite the challenges and hardships they face, these survivors manage to overcome adversity and make it back to land.
